steps.prompt: Which word is the main finite verb?

hints.agente: 'Agente' is a present participle meaning 'doing/performing' in the ablative
hints.Gaudium: 'Gaudium' is a noun meaning 'joy' — the subject
hints.angelis: 'Angelis' is a noun meaning 'angels' in the ablative/dative

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sit' — 'erit' is future indicative

hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.erro, errare, erravi, erratum: These are the parts of 'errare' (to wander)

hints.perfect passive participle: A perfect passive participle would end in -to/-ta — 'agente' ends in -ente
hints.future active participle: A future active participle would end in -uro/-ura — 'agente' is present
hints.gerundive: A gerundive would end in -endo/-enda — 'agente' is a present participle

steps.prompt: What construction is 'uno peccatore paenitentiam agente'?
steps.reveal: = with one sinner doing penance / when one sinner repents

hints.prepositional phrase: There is no preposition governing this phrase — it is an ablative absolute
hints.indirect statement: Indirect statement uses accusative + infinitive — this uses ablative + participle
hints.relative clause: A relative clause would use 'qui/quae/quod' — this is a participial construction in the ablative

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'angeli' — 'angelis' is dative/ablative
hints.dative: 'Angelis' could be dative, but after 'coram' (in the presence of) it is ablative
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'angelorum' — 'angelis' is ablative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'angeli' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'angeli' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'angeli' ends in -i

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'paenitentia' — the -am ending marks accusative

steps.prompt: The phrase 'paenitentiam agere' is an idiom meaning?

hints.to drive out repentance: 'Agere' can mean 'to drive' but 'paenitentiam agere' is an idiom for 'to repent'
hints.to act with regret: While close in meaning, the standard translation of this idiom is 'to do penance/repent'
hints.to lead a penitent: 'Agere' does not mean 'to lead a person' — the idiom means 'to repent'

translation: There will be joy before the angels of God over one sinner repenting
